Swiss CitiesLeukerbad

Leukerbad

VSValais

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The water that remembers

A child dips into the thermal pool and the water shows visions of its 40-year journey: falling as snow on the peaks, sinking through rock layers, heating in the deep earth, rising to the surface. The child follows the water's memory back through time, discovering the ancient geological forces that transform Alpine snowflakes into healing warmth beneath a village of stone and steam.

Create This Story

Leukerbad is the largest thermal spa resort in the Alps, with 65 thermal springs delivering over 3.9 million litres of water per day at temperatures up to 51 degrees Celsius. The water begins as precipitation at 2,300-3,000 metres altitude, seeps underground through porous limestone, descends to approximately 500 metres below sea level, is heated by geothermal energy and enriched with calcium and sulphate, then rises back to the surface — a journey of over 40 years.

The mule on the Gemmi Wall

A stubborn mule on the 1741 cliff path refuses to move, blocking the entire trail high above Leukerbad. A child must befriend the frightened animal and guide it down the terrifying zigzags while traders, pilgrims, and porters wait impatiently above, each with their own urgent reason to cross the Gemmi before nightfall.

Create This Story

The Gemmi Pass (2,346 m) links Leukerbad in the Valais with Kandersteg in the Bernese Oberland. The southern descent drops nearly 900 metres down the sheer Gemmiwand — a vertical rock wall approximately 550 metres high. In 1739-1741, Tyrolean workmen carved the current mule path into the cliff face in extraordinary zigzags. Mark Twain crossed the pass in 1878, noting 'the mule prefers the outside' — forcing walkers to press against the cliff wall.

Baldwin's typewriter

A child exploring an old Leukerbad chalet finds a vintage typewriter that types stories on its own. Each page reveals a scene from the village's past through the eyes of a stranger — echoing Baldwin's experience of being the first outsider to see this world differently. It is a story about belonging, difference, and the power of writing to bridge worlds.

Create This Story

James Baldwin, the African-American novelist, first came to Leukerbad in 1951 at age 27, brought by his partner, Swiss painter Lucien Happersberger. Baldwin spent the winters of 1951-1952 and 1952-1953 in near-total isolation, completing his first novel Go Tell It on the Mountain (1953). He was the first Black person many villagers had ever seen. He channelled these experiences into the landmark essay 'Stranger in the Village,' published in Harper's Magazine in October 1953. In 2018, Swiss-Haitian artist Sasha Huber installed a portrait of Baldwin made of metal staples on his former chalet.

Twain's corkscrew

Mark Twain's hat blows off the Gemmi precipice and a child chases it down the spiralling cliff path, tumbling through chapters of history at every switchback. At each turn they encounter a different literary pilgrim — Roman traders, medieval pilgrims, Goethe sketching the view, Picasso signing the guest book — in a dizzying descent through centuries of Alpine storytelling.

Create This Story

Mark Twain crossed the Gemmi Pass in the summer of 1878, describing the descent as 'the most remarkable road I have ever seen' with 'corkscrew curves down the face of the colossal precipice.' Johann Wolfgang von Goethe had visited Leukerbad in 1779, Pablo Picasso crossed the Gemmi in 1933 and signed the Schwarenbach guest books, and Guy de Maupassant visited the Schwarenbach Inn in 1877, later writing the chilling story 'The Inn' (L'Auberge, 1886).

The cardinal's cure

Cardinal Schiner, exhausted from wars and papal politics, rides to Leukerbad to heal his weary body. A village child becomes his unlikely guide to the springs and shows him that the water's real magic is not in curing the body but in slowing down — a story about the most powerful man in the Valais learning gentleness from the humblest source.

Create This Story

Matthaeus Schiner (c. 1465-1522), born to peasant parents in Upper Valais, rose to become Bishop of Sion in 1499 and Cardinal in 1511. In 1501, he acquired the rights to the hot springs in Leukerbad, invested in the baths, and had a magnificent inn built — marking the beginning of modern spa tourism. His career was extraordinary: he served as Papal Minister of War, directed Swiss mercenary forces in the Italian Wars, and nearly became Pope before French cardinals blocked his candidacy. He died of plague in Rome in 1522.

The avalanche wall

In 1718, an avalanche buries the entire village of Leukerbad. A child and a mountain dog must dig through snow to find survivors, guided by the faint warmth of the thermal springs seeping up through the frozen ground. Together they race against time, following the heat rising from deep below — the same ancient water that has drawn people here for two thousand years.

Create This Story

Leukerbad's position beneath towering cliffs has made it vulnerable to avalanches for centuries. In 1518, an avalanche killed 61 people. On 17 January 1718, a massive avalanche destroyed every house in the village, killing 52 people. Between 1829 and 1831, the Werimauer was constructed — considered Switzerland's first avalanche wall, a massive stone barrier to deflect flows. The village's repeated destruction and rebuilding is a testament to the stubbornness of the Valaisan people and the irresistible draw of the thermal springs.

Maupassant's ghost at the Schwarenbach

A child stranded overnight at the lonely Schwarenbach Inn on the Gemmi hears strange sounds in the snow. The inn's guest book comes alive with stories from Maupassant, Twain, and Picasso, each warning about a different danger — but only the child can figure out which story is real and which is fiction before the mountain night closes in.

Create This Story

Guy de Maupassant visited the Schwarenbach Inn on the Gemmi in 1877 and later wrote the chilling short story 'The Inn' (L'Auberge, 1886) — a tale of two guides driven mad by isolation and snow. Mark Twain described the inn as 'on a lonely spot among the peaks, swept by the cloud rack.' Pablo Picasso crossed the Gemmi in 1933 and signed the Schwarenbach guest books. The inn sits high on the pass near the dark waters of the Daubensee.

The rust-red river

A child following the iron-stained waters through the Dala Gorge discovers a hidden spring that no one has found before. The water is warmer and older than any other, and it holds the memory of the very first people who bathed here in Roman times — a journey deep into the moss-covered canyon where the earth breathes heat and the rocks tell stories in streaks of rust.

Create This Story

The Dalaschlucht (Dala Gorge) begins less than one kilometre from the village square — a narrow, moss-covered canyon carved by the Dala River. The Thermal Springs Footbridge spans approximately 600 metres through the gorge, where thermal water seeps from cracks, leaving rust-brown streaks from the iron content. A 21-metre bridge crosses to a 35-metre-high waterfall. The gorge is where the underground thermal waters first become visible — where the 40-year journey of the water can be witnessed at the surface.
